MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-11-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. WILLIAM W. PALMER, ESQ. of the Palmer Law Group, Tel No. 916-972-0761 appearing telephonically for the Plaintiffs;
No other appearances made in this matter.

RULING - Matter argued by Plaintiffs' counsel. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT BETTY YEE'S MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT is granted. On December 4, 2014, the Court issued an order sustaining Defendant's demurrer to the Second Amended Complaint. The order states: "Qualified immunity shields Mr. Chiang from liability as an individual. 'Government officials performing discretionary functions generally are shielded from liability for civil damages insofar as their conduct does not violate clearly established statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known.' (Harlow v. Fitzgerald (1982) 457 U.S. 800, 818.) The Second Amended Complaint ("SAC") pleads that Mr. Chiang complied with the provisions of the UPL. (SAC, pars. 15, 20.) The Ninth Circuit upheld the new escheatment statutory scheme as Constitutional on its face. (Taylor v. Westly (9th Cir. 2008) 525 F.3d 1288, 1289 ("Taylor III").) Because the Complaint does not allege conduct outside the parameters of the escheatment statutes, a reasonable person would not have known that his conduct would violate Constitutional rights. Although the section 1983 action against Mr. Chiang individually fails, Plaintiff may be able to amend the SAC to state a cause of action against the State directly to the extent it seeks damages equal to the amount of the property held in trust only or an injunction. (See Taylor v. Westly (9th Cir. 2005) 402 F.3d 924, 932-35 ("Taylor I"); see also Suever v. Connell (9th Cir. 2009) 579 F.3d 1047, 1059, 'Our case law states that the Eleventh Amendment does not bar claims by plaintiffs for the return of their own property under the UPL, because such claims are not for 'damages' against the State.'' The demurrer to the first cause of action for Declaratory Relief is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. There may be a basis for a declaratory relief cause of action, but its contours would have to be consistent with the ruling on the demurrers to the other causes of action." In his Third Amended Complaint, Plaintiff has not alleged any cause of action against the State directly. Further, the basis for declaratory relief is not consistent with the ruling on demurrer, therefore Defendant's motion to strike the Third Amended Complaint is granted. Prevailing party shall prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-25-2014 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
DEFENDANT JOHN CHIANG'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT - The demurrer to the Second Cause of Action for Violation of 42 U.S.C. sec. 1983 is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Qualified immunity shields Mr. Chiang from liability as an individual. "Government officials performing discretionary functions generally are shielded from liability for civil damages insofar as their conduct does not violate clearly established statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known." (Harlow v. Fitzgerald (1982) 457 U.S. 800, 818.) The Second Amended Complaint ("SAC") pleads that Mr. Chiang complied with the provisions of the UPL. (SAC, pars. 15, 20.) The Ninth Circuit upheld the new escheatment statutory scheme as Constitutional on its face. (Taylor v. Westly (9th Cir. 2008) 525 F.3d 1288, 1289 ("Taylor III").) Because the Complaint does not allege conduct outside the parameters of the escheatment statutes, a reasonable person would not have known that his conduct would violate Constitutional rights. Although the section 1983 action against Mr. Chiang individually fails, Plaintiff may be able to amend the SAC to state a cause of action against the State directly to the extent it seeks damages equal to the amount of the property held in trust only or an injunction. (See Taylor v. Westly (9th Cir. 2005) 402 F.3d 924, 932-35 ("Taylor I"); see also Suever v. Connell (9th Cir. 2009) 579 F.3d 1047, 1059, "Our case law states that the Eleventh Amendment does not bar claims by plaintiffs for the return of their own property under the UPL, because such claims are not for 'damages' against the State.") The demurrer to the third cause of action for Violation of CCP secs. 1300 et seq. is sustained without leave to amend. Although the goal of the UPL is to reunite escheated property with its owner, there is a specific statute, Section 1530(b)(1), providing that identifying information does not have to be collected for property valued at less than $50. (SAC, par. 15.) When a conflict exists between a general and a specific law, the provisions of the specific law prevail. (CCP 1859; see also e.g. People v. Superior Court (2002) 28 Cal.4th 798, 808.) The Complaint pleads that Mr. Chiang complied with Section 1530(b)(1). (SAC, par. 20.) The demurrer to the first cause of action for Declaratory Relief is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. There may be a basis for a declaratory relief cause of action, but its contours would have to be consistent with the ruling on the demurrers to the other causes of action. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sheryl L. Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/ehg
